Learn about your taxes

## Completing a basic tax return
An introduction to a basic income tax and benefit return. What you need to report, how to claim deductions and tax
    credits, and finding out whether you will get a refund or owe tax.

### Filling out your tax return
There are 6 steps to completing your income tax and benefit return. These steps are
                                    the same for simple tax situations and complex ones. Knowing the basic steps and
                                    seeing how they connect will help you do your taxes, whether you use a paper return
                                    or file electronically using certified tax software.
If you do your taxes electronically, please note that the certified tax software you
                                    use may not show all the individual steps. The software calculates continuously as
                                    you enter more information.
Learn all about the basics of a tax return
Launch video : The one about doing your taxes

#### The one about doing your taxes
Alternative link for video opens in a new
                                    window

### The 6 steps explained

#### Step 1: Identification and other information
Enter personal information about yourself so the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) can identify you
                            and accurately calculate the benefits and credits you may be entitled to.

#### Step 2: Total income
Report your income from all sources that you earned or received during the year. The taxes
                            you might owe are calculated based on your income.
If you have tax-exempt income
If you are registered or entitled to be registered under the Indian Act , you may have earned
                                or received income that is tax-exempt income under section 87 of the Indian Act . Do not
                                include this income in Step 2 of your tax return. Instead, fill out Form T90, Income Exempt
                                from Tax under the Indian Act , to calculate your net exempt income and submit it with your
                                tax return.
Note : The CRA uses the term "Indian" because it has a legal meaning in the Indian Act .
Claim various deductions to reduce your total income to your net income.
The calculation of your taxable income is step 4 of your tax return.

#### Step 4: Taxable income
Claim other deductions to further reduce your net income to your taxable income.
- = equals Taxable income
Your taxable income is used to calculate how much federal and provincial or territorial tax you may have to pay.
Calculate the federal tax you owe on your taxable income by using your taxable income, the
                            applicable tax rate and the non‑refundable tax credits you're entitled to.
- × multiplied by Federal tax rate
- − minus Federal
                                non-refundable tax credits
- = equals Federal tax
Note : Federal tax will show up as Net federal tax on the paper return.
You will do a similar calculation to figure out the provincial or territorial taxes you
                                might owe. You will report this amount in Step 6 .
Note : If you lived in Quebec on December 31 of the tax year, you will
                                calculate your provincial taxes by completing a separate provincial tax return for
                                Revenu Québec.

#### Step 6: Refund or balance owing
Determine the final result of your tax return by subtracting your total credits from your
                            total payable. You will either be entitled to a refund or have a balance owing.
- − minus Total credits
- = equals Refund or balance
                                owing

### Filing your tax return electronically
There are many certified tax software you can choose from to do your taxes, some of which are
                            free, and each is a bit different. When using a software, you will answer some questions at
                            the start of the process about your personal situation. Some software makes suggestions based
                            on the questions you answered, while others do not provide suggestions and require you to
                            enter certain amounts yourself.
Most software guide you through the process so you do not miss out on any deductions, credits
                            or benefits that you may be entitled to. However, it is always a good idea to know what is
                            available to make sure you are not missing out.
If you are using certified tax software, it will do the calculations for you automatically
                            and may not show the individual steps.
Most software advise you of any issues or errors with your tax return so you can correct them
                            before submitting the return to the CRA. Although the software does the calculations for
                            you, you are ultimately responsible for the tax return that you submit, so it is important
                            to understand how the calculations are completed.
Resources are available
After you finish this lesson, these resource links will be available:
- Find certified tax software
- Claiming deductions, credits and expenses

### Doing your taxes every year
It is very important to do your taxes every year, even if you had no income in the
                                year .
The CRA uses the information from your taxes to determine if you are entitled to a tax refund
                            or any benefit and credit payments, like the goods and services tax/harmonized sales tax
                            (GST/HST) credit or the Canada child benefit.
Other government departments also use information from your tax return to calculate other
                            payments. For example, Service Canada uses the information to calculate the guaranteed
                            income supplement (GIS) for adults 65 or older. Not filing your taxes by the due date will
                            interrupt your GIS payments.
If you are registered for a CRA account and are using certified tax software, you can use
                                Auto-fill my return. This service lets you automatically fill in parts of your tax
                                return with information that the CRA has available. This means you don't have to
                                manually enter all of the information from your slips.
When using this service, make sure the CRA has all of your slips on file and the
                                information is correct.

### Why provide information about yourself
When you fill out Step 1 of your income tax and benefit return, you must provide your personal identification and other information. This is
                            so the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) can identify you and accurately calculate the benefits
                            and credits you may be entitled to.
The CRA uses the information you provide in Step 1 of your tax return to communicate with you and to calculate certain benefits. Incorrect or incomplete information can cause delays or inaccurate calculations. It is important to provide the information to the best of
                            your knowledge.
The CRA ensures your information is kept confidential.
The CRA will not ask for information about your passport, health card,
                                or driver’s licence.

#### Updating your information with the CRA before you file your taxes electronically
You cannot update certain personal information, such as your address
                            or name, by entering the new information into the certified tax software. Be sure to
                            update this information with the CRA before you use the software.
You may be able to update this information using My Account. By registering for a CRA account, you can access helpful services to complete your tax return.
You can also contact the CRA to make changes to your personal information.
Resources are available
After you finish this lesson, this resource link will be available:
- Update your personal information with the CRA

### Providing your name, address, and social insurance number
You must provide your first and last name. If you are using certified tax software, the name you put on your tax return must match what
                            is on file with the CRA.
To change your name, please contact the CRA by phone or by mail.
Example: Reporting your name
Charles Bélanger-Chapleau filed his first tax return using his
                                full name. He must use this same name every year, unless he contacts the CRA to
                                change it. Using only "Charles Bélanger " may create problems in
                                the CRA's system.
You must provide your complete mailing address. You can update your mailing address on My
                            Account or by contacting the CRA.

#### Social insurance number (SIN)
You must provide your SIN, which is a 9-digit number (for example, 111 111
                                111 ) that identifies you for income tax purposes. It also allows you to work in
                            Canada and receive benefits and services from the government.
Your SIN is unique, personal, and confidential. You must provide your SIN to your
                            employer, your financial institution, and certain government departments.
Provide your individual tax number (ITN) or temporary tax number (TTN) if you received
                                one because you are not eligible for a SIN. Contact the CRA if you need to apply for an
                                ITN or a TTN.
Contact Service Canada if you are eligible for a SIN but do not have one.
Resources are available
After you finish this lesson, these resource links will be available:
- Personal and address information
- Contact the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA)

### Providing your date of birth, language of correspondence, and email
                            address
You must provide your complete date of birth. The CRA uses your date of birth to check your
                            identity.
The CRA must have your complete date of birth on record for you to submit your return
                            electronically. You can contact the CRA to confirm what information is on file before doing
                            your taxes electronically.

#### Language of correspondence
You must choose either English or French. The CRA communicates with you in the official
                            language of your choice.
If you have a CRA account and want to receive your CRA correspondence electronically include your email address on your tax return to register for
                            “Electronic mail.” You can also set your correspondence preferences to “Electronic mail” in
                            your My Account. If you choose “Electronic mail”, the CRA will email you to tell you when
                            correspondence is waiting for you in your My Account.
If your correspondence preference is set to “Electronic mail” but you want to continue receiving your correspondence by mail, you can unselect your correspondence preference in My Account.
If you don’t have access to a CRA account and prefer to receive your correspondence by
                                mail, don’t include your email address on your tax return.

### Providing your province or territory of residence
You must indicate what your province or territory of residence was on December 31 of the tax
                            year. This is usually the province or territory where you were living, but it may be
                            different if you were living away from your usual home.
Example: Living away from your usual home
Lucie usually resides in Nova Scotia but was going to school in Alberta. They should
                                indicate that their province of residence was Nova Scotia.
Your province or territory of residence determines what benefits and credits are available to
                            you and what provincial or territorial tax rate applies to you. If you are using certified tax software, it will fill
                            out the appropriate forms and schedules for your province or territory of residence based on
                            information you enter as you complete your return.

### Providing your marital status
You must indicate what your marital status was on December 31 of the tax year. This
                            information is used to determine your eligibility for certain benefits and tax credits and
                            to calculate the amounts you may be entitled to.
You have a spouse. This term only applies to a person you are legally married to.
You live in a conjugal relationship with another person who is not your married spouse, and at least
                            one of the following conditions applies:
- This person has been living with you in a conjugal relationship for at least 12 continuous months Note : in this definition, 12 continuous months includes any
                                        period where you were separated for less than 90 days because of a breakdown in
                                        the relationship
- Note : in this definition, 12 continuous months includes any
                                        period where you were separated for less than 90 days because of a breakdown in
                                        the relationship
- This person is the parent of your child by birth or adoption
- This person has custody and control of your child (or had custody and control
                                immediately before the child turned 19 years of age), and your child is wholly dependent on this person for support
Your spouse or common-law partner is now deceased.
You are legally divorced from your former spouse and are not in a relationship that would result in a new marital status on December 31.
You have been living separate and apart from your spouse or common-law partner because of a breakdown in your relationship for a period of at least 90 consecutive days.
Note : You are still considered to have a spouse or common-law partner if you were separated involuntarily and not because of a breakdown in your relationship. An involuntary separation could happen when one spouse or common-law partner is living away for work, school or health reasons, or is incarcerated.
None of the other marital statuses apply to you.
Cheyenne and Abigail lived together as roommates for several years. They both reported their
                                marital status as single on their tax returns because they did not live together in a conjugal relationship.
Example: Birth of your child
Malaia and Ariyan moved in together in October and are in a conjugal relationship. They were considered common-law in November as soon as their baby was born. They updated their marital status to common-law with the CRA.
Example: Living separate and apart to attend school
Embry and Darcy have been married for several years. Darcy is attending post-secondary education in New Brunswick and Embry is working and raising their children in Saskatchewan. They both report their marital statuses as married on their tax returns because Darcy is living in New Brunswick to attend school. They are not living separate and apart due to a breakdown in a relationship.

### Registering with Elections Canada
You must indicate if you have Canadian citizenship and, if so, whether you want the CRA to
                            share your information with Elections Canada.
The CRA will only share your:
If you are 18 years of age or older and you check “Yes” to both questions, your name and up-to-date voter registration will be added to the National Register of Electors.
If you are between the ages of 14 to 17 and you check “Yes” to both questions, your name will be added to the Register of Future Electors. Once you turn 18 and your eligibility to vote is confirmed, you will be added to the National Register of Electors.
You must be at least 18 years old and have Canadian citizenship to vote in a
                                federal election.
Example: Registering with Elections Canada
Meadow is 16 and filing their first tax return this year. Meadow checked off yes to both questions about Elections Canada. Their name will be added to the Register of Future Electors and when they turn 18, Elections Canada will add their name to the National Register of Electors.
Resources are available
After you finish this lesson, this resource link will be available:

### Other questions you may need to answer

#### Entering or leaving Canada
If you became a resident of Canada in the tax year, you must provide your date of entry. If
                            you left Canada in the tax year and became a resident of another country, you must provide your date of
                            departure.

#### Residing on the settlement lands of Indigenous governments
In some provinces and territories, you are required to answer questions to
                            identify if you resided on the settlement lands of an Indigenous government on
                            December 31 of the tax year.
These Indigenous governments are:
- Nunatsiavut Government (in Newfoundland and Labrador )
- Tłı̨chǫ (Tlicho) Government and Délı̨nę Got’ı̨nę Government (in the Northwest
                                    Territories )
- 11 self-governing Yukon First Nations
Only the tax return for the provinces or territories that are listed above will include
                            questions about residence on settlement lands.
You must let the CRA know whether you have any income that is exempt from tax under section 87 of the Indian Act .
If you check this box, you must fill out Form T90, Income Exempt from Tax under the Indian
                            Act, so the CRA can calculate your Canada workers benefit, if it applies, and your family's
                            provincial or territorial benefits. The CRA will also use the information you provide on
                            Form T90 to calculate your Canada training credit limit.
Note : The CRA uses the term "Indian" because it has a legal meaning in the Indian Act .
Resources are available
After you finish this lesson, this resource link will be available:
- Taxes and benefits for Indigenous Peoples

### Entering personal information on your tax return
If you are using certified tax software, you enter your personal information at the start of
                            the process. Based on the responses you provide, the software determines if more information
                            is required before moving to the next step. The software may also use the information you
                            provide in this section to determine if you are eligible for certain tax credits or if other
                            forms need to be completed.
Example: How your personal information can determine eligibility
While doing her taxes using certified tax software, Ruman indicated she is married and a
                                resident of Ontario. Her marital status prompted the software to ask her for her
                                spouse’s SIN and net income. Her province of residence prompted the software to ask
                                additional questions related to Form ON-BEN, Application for the Ontario Trillium
                                Benefit, and Ontario Senior Homeowners’ Property Tax Grant, because this form applies
                                only to residents of Ontario.
Below is an example of what the identification and other information page looks like on the
                            tax return:
Is this form hard to read? Don't worry, we will provide you with a
                                link to the PDF (hi-res) version after this lesson.

Income is amounts you earn or receive during the tax year through salary, social benefits,
                            property, investments or professional activities. You could also receive amounts other than
                            your salary, such as tips or occasional earnings from work you do on the
                            side.
Example: What is considered income
Malika has a summer job as a food delivery driver. The money she earns, including tips,
                                is considered her income.
In Step 2 of your income tax and benefit return, you must report your income
                            from all sources for the tax year. This includes income from all sources inside and outside
                            Canada. The total of these amounts is known as your total income.
The taxes that you might owe are calculated based on your income. The Canada Revenue Agency
                            (CRA) operates several review programs to make sure that your income is reported correctly.
                            If you do not report all of your income, you may have to pay penalties and interest as well
                            as the tax you should have paid on the missing income.
Tips controlled by your employer will be reported on your tax slip as “employment
                                income.” You need to report cash tips given to you directly as “other employment
                                income.”
Resources are available

- Review of your tax return

### Information slips explained
Most income you receive will be reported on an information slip, sometimes called a tax
                            slip. This is a tax document given to you by whoever paid you the income, such as your
                            employer or financial institution. Your income information is reported on the slip in
                            numbered boxes that each refer to a specific line on the tax return. Each information slip has instructions about where to report the amounts on your tax return.
Different types of income are reported on different slips, so you might receive multiple
                            slips if you had more than one source of income.
Even though not all your income may be reported on an information slip, such as tips or
                            occasional earnings, you are still responsible for reporting it on your tax return. You
                            should keep track of all the money you earn throughout the year, especially tips that are
                            not recorded on your paystubs, so you are ready at tax time.
The information slips listed below are used to report the basic types of income,
                                deductions and tax credits on a tax return.

#### Common information slips
This slip shows your employment income earned as an employee. The boxes on the T4 slip
                                should show the same year-to-date amounts as on the last pay stub you received at the
                                end of the year.
Compare your T4 slip to that paystub to make sure the amounts are
                                the same. If you notice any differences, you should discuss them with your employer and
                                resolve the situation before contacting the CRA.
Example of a T4 slip
Is this slip hard to read? Don't worry, we will provide you with a
                                link to the PDF (hi-res) version after this lesson.
This slip shows several different types of income, such as:
- payments from a registered education savings plan (RESP)
- fees or commissions for self-employed individuals
Example of a T4A slip
Is this slip hard to read? Don't worry, we will provide you with a
                                link to the PDF (hi-res) version after this lesson.
Service Canada uses this slip to report benefits they send, such as
                                employment insurance (EI) benefits and tuition assistance.
Example of a T4E slip
Is this slip hard to read? Don't worry, we will provide you with a
                                link to the PDF (hi-res) version after this lesson.
This slip shows the following types of income:
- workers’ compensation benefits
- social assistance payments
- provincial or territorial supplements
Example of a T5007 slip
Is this slip hard to read? Don't worry, we will provide you with a
                                link to the PDF (hi-res) version after this lesson.
- construction subcontractor payments.
Example of a T5018 slip
Is this slip hard to read? Don't worry, we will provide you with a
                                link to the PDF (hi-res) version after this lesson.
If you work and live in the province of Quebec, you will also receive Relevé slips.
                                    You cannot access your Relevé slips through My Account with the CRA. The provincial taxes for the province of Quebec are governed by Revenu
                                    Québec.
Resources are available

### When and how you get your information slips
Most issuers must send you your slip by the end of February following the tax year. They can
                            send you a physical or digital copy, but both formats contain the same information. If you
                            have an online account with the issuer, you may be able to view your slips online.
Example: Getting a T4 from your employers
Elijah worked for 2 different employers last year. Elijah knew his employment income
                                would be reported on a T4 slip, Statement of Remuneration Paid. Because Elijah worked
                                for more than one employer during the year, he will receive a T4 slip from each of them.
The issuer must send a copy of your slip to the CRA. This means you can also access your
                            slips through My Account, if you are registered for a CRA account. You can register for a CRA account after you have filed your taxes for the first time.
Sorry,
                                                        that's incorrect.
Kasem will receive a paystub for the last shifts he worked, but his
                                                    employer is only required to send him his T4 slip by the end of
                                                    February following the tax year.
If he did not receive his T4 slip from his employer, he can check My Account to
                                                    view the copy that was submitted to the CRA.

                                                        correct.
Kasem will receive a paystub for the last shifts he worked, but his
                                                    employer is only required to send him his T4 slip by the end of
                                                    February following the tax year.
If he did not receive his T4 slip from his employer, he can check My Account to
                                                    view the copy that was submitted to the CRA.

### Common types of income
You’ll receive a T4 slip if you earned or received income from your employer, such as salary,
                            wages, commissions, bonuses, or tips. You will see the amount to
                            report on your tax return in box 14 of your T4 slip.

#### Other employment income, including direct tips
This amount most commonly includes cash tips paid directly to you because you won’t receive
                            an information slip for them. This amount also includes occasional earnings, such as
                            from babysitting or other odd jobs.
If you are self-employed and earn income through independent work, that income is not
                            reported in this section. For more information on being self-employed, take the module: Working for yourself .
If you are registered or entitled to be registered under the Indian Act , you may have earned or received tax-exempt employment or self-employment income that will be reported on a T4 slip. You will see the amounts in boxes 71 or 88. You should not report these amounts on your tax return. Instead, report them on Form T90, Income Exempt from Tax under the Indian Act . The CRA will use the information you give on Form T90 to calculate certain benefits and credits.
Note : The Canada Revenue Agency uses the term “Indian” because it has a legal meaning in the Indian Act .
For more information, see “Tax-exempt income” in part 6 of 8 of this lesson.
You are responsible for reporting all income earned or received, including in cash, even if
                            you did not receive an information slip.

#### Employment insurance and other benefits
If you received benefits from Service Canada, such as EI or tuition assistance, you’ll
                            receive a T4E slip. You will see the amount to report on your tax return in box 14.

#### Interest and other investment income
If you received this type of income from your bank or financial institution, you’ll usually
                            receive a T5 slip, Statement of Investment Income. It uses various box numbers to report
                            income.
You may not receive a T5 slip if the amount is less than $50, but you must
                            still report the income. You should check your financial statements to confirm if you
                            received this type of income during the year.

#### Scholarships, fellowships, bursaries, and artists' project grants
If you received any of these amounts during the year, you will receive a T4A slip. The full
                            payments you received are shown in box 105. However, you may not need to report the entire
                            amount on your tax return, depending on the type of scholarship received, what kind of
                            education program you are enrolled in, and whether you are in school full-time or part-time.
If you are a part-time student who is eligible for the disability tax credit you may not
                                need to report your scholarship on your tax return.
Resources are available
After you finish this lesson, these resource links will be available:
- Line 10400 – Other employment income
- Line 13010 – Taxable scholarships, fellowships, bursaries, and artists' project grants

### More common types of income

#### Payments from a Registered Education Savings Plan (RESP)
If you received taxable payments from an RESP during the year, you will receive a T4A slip.
                            Educational assistance payments (EAP) are shown in box 042 and accumulated income payments
                            (AIP) are shown in box 040. A refund of contributions is not shown on a slip, as these
                            payments are not taxable and do not need to be reported on your tax return.

#### Social assistance payments
If you received social assistance, from your provincial or territorial government, you’ll receive a T5007 slip. The amount to report on your tax return is in box 11.
While these payments are not taxable, you must report them to make sure any benefits you may
                            be entitled to are calculated correctly. You will claim an offsetting deduction for the same
                            amount in step 3 so you don’t pay any tax on this income. If you are
                            using certified tax software, it will automatically calculate the amount of this offsetting
                            deduction.

#### Social assistance payments from a First Nation or band council
If you are registered or entitled to be registered under the Indian Act and you received social assistance payments from your First Nation or band council, you do not need to pay tax on those payments. Fill in the amount you received on Form T90, Income Exempt from Tax under the Indian Act . These payments are tax-exempt so don’t include them in Step 2 of the tax return.
For more information, see “Tax-exempt income” in part 6 of 8 of this lesson.

#### Income from the platform economy
The platform economy includes income earned through online activities such as sharing
                            accommodation, buying and selling or providing freelance services. Income can also be earned
                            through revenue from advertisements or product promotion through videos or pictures. If you
                            participate in the platform economy, you may be self-employed.
You must report all income you earn when you are self-employed on your tax return, including:
- cash transactions with clients
- barter transactions, where you exchange goods or services with another business
If you are unsure if you are an employee or a self-employed individual, and whether your
                                employment is pensionable and insurable, you can ask the CRA to complete a CPP/EI
                                ruling. For more information on CPP/EI rulings, take the lesson: The differences
                                    between being an employee and a self-employed individual .
There is a self-employment income section on the tax return where you should report this
                            amount. You will also need to fill out Form T2125, Statement of Business or Professional
                            Activities, to calculate your self-employment business and professional income. If you are
                            using a NETFILE-certified tax software, it will ask you the necessary questions to automatically
                            fill out this form for you.
For more information on being self-employed, take the module: Working for
                                yourself .

### Other income situations
It is important to do your taxes every year, even if your exempt income is not reported on your tax return . This is true for anyone registered or entitled to be registered as an Indian under the Indian Act with tax-exempt income under section 87 of the Indian Act . This lets the CRA determine if you are entitled to a tax refund or to any benefit and credit payments.
You may also need to complete Form T90, Income Exempt from Tax Under the Indian Act , so the CRA can calculate your Canada training credit limit and your Canada workers benefit, if applicable. This form may also be used to calculate your family’s provincial or territorial benefits.
Note : The CRA uses the term “Indian” because it has a legal meaning in the Indian Act .

#### Amounts that are not taxed
Certain amounts earned or received are non-taxable. You do not need to report these as income
                            on your tax return. Non‑taxable amounts include most lottery winnings, most gifts and
                            inheritances, and payments from programs like the GST/HST credit and the Canada child
                            benefit.
Example: Money received for your birthday
Benoît's grandparents gave him $100 for his birthday. This amount
                                does not need to be reported as income on Benoît's tax return
                                because it is not taxable and is not used to calculate his benefits and credits.

#### Individuals with no income
If you did not make any money during the year, you do not need to enter any information in
                            Step 2 of the tax return.
However, it is very important to do your taxes every year, even if you had no income
                                in the year , so the CRA can determine if you are entitled to a tax refund or
                            any benefit and credit payments.

### Deductions explained
Deductions are certain expenses and other amounts that you subtract from your total income or
                            your net income when you fill out your income tax and benefit return. Because the taxes you
                            owe are calculated using your income, deductions reduce the amount of tax that you may have
                            to pay. Some deductions reduce your net income and others reduce your taxable income.
The calculation of your net income is step 3 of your tax return.
The Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) uses your net income to calculate the benefits and credits
                            you may be eligible for, such as the Canada child benefit and the GST/HST credit. Your net
                            income is also used to calculate certain tax credits in a later step on the tax return.
The calculation of your taxable income is step 4 of your tax return.

#### Step 4: Taxable income

                                        income
Your taxable income is used to calculate how much federal and provincial or territorial tax
                            you may have to pay.
If you own a small business or are self-employed, you may be able to deduct business
                            expenses. For more information on business-related expenses, take the lesson: Reporting your self-employment income .
If you are using a certified tax software, it does these calculations for you automatically.

                                step 3 of the tax return, it reduces Haru’s net income to $21,700 ($22,000 total income
                                minus the $300 deduction). Haru does not claim any deductions in step 4 of the tax
                                return, so his taxable income is also $21,700.

### Common types of deductions

#### Registered pension plan (RPP) contributions
An RPP is a pension plan that your employer sets up and registers with the CRA. The purpose
                            of an RPP is to provide you with a pension when you retire.
Usually, your employer deducts your RPP contributions from your pay cheque on a regular
                            basis, and the total amount is shown in box 20 of your T4 slip. It may also appear on other
                            receipts, as well as in box 32 of a T4A slip, Statement of Pension, Retirement, Annuity, and
                            Other Income.
If you are using certified tax software, you enter the amount shown on your information slips
                            or receipts, and the software enters the total on the correct line.

#### Registered retirement savings plan (RRSP) contributions
An RRSP is a retirement savings and investment plan that individuals can open and contribute to. Deductible RRSP contributions can be used to reduce your income tax.
Your financial institution reports the amount of your contributions on a contribution
                            receipt.
You will need to know the amount of your contributions and your RRSP deduction limit for the
                            year. You can find your RRSP deduction limit on your My Account or on your latest notice of
                            assessment. If you are using certified tax software, the software may ask you if you want to
                            claim a deduction for the contributions. It will then calculate (on a Schedule 7, RRSP,
                            PRPP, and SPP Unused Contributions, transfers, and HBP or LLP Activities) your deduction and
                            enter it on the correct line.

                            activities. Usually, your employer deducts your union dues from your pay cheque on a regular
                            basis, and the total amount is shown in box 44 of a T4 slip. It may also appear on other
                            receipts.
If you are using certified tax software, you will enter the amount shown on your slips or
                            receipts, and the software will enter the sum on the correct line.

Non-refundable tax credits are amounts you claim to lower the amount of taxes you might owe.

### More about non-refundable tax credits
Some non-refundable tax credits are based on your personal situation, while others are
                            related to your employment income or expenses you may have paid during the year.
The value of each non-refundable tax credit varies between tax years. They are adjusted each
                            year to allow for inflation and other factors.
The total of your non-refundable tax credits is multiplied by the rate that each federal,
                            provincial, or territorial government apply on non-refundable tax credits. This means that
                            you can claim a certain percentage of each credit. This percentage is pre-determined by each
                            government. The resulting amount lowers the taxes you owe on your income.
Example: Calculating your non-refundable tax credits
Isaiah can claim $20,000 of federal non-refundable tax credits. The federal
                                non-refundable tax credit rate is 15%. The amount that will lower the taxes he owes on
                                his income is calculated like this: $20,000 × multiplied by 15% = equals $3,000
If Isaiah owes $4,000 of federal tax on his income, his non‑refundable tax credits will
                                reduce this amount: $4,000 − minus $3,000 = equals $1,000

### Common types of non-refundable tax credits
Some non-refundable tax credits may be less if you are not a resident of Canada for the
                            entire tax year. This page covers the non-refundable tax credits as if you were a resident
                            for the entire tax year.

#### Basic personal amount
All individuals are eligible for this credit, and it is automatically calculated for you if
                            you are using certified tax software. If your taxable income is below the basic personal
                            amount, the taxes you owe on your income are reduced to zero.

#### Spouse or common-law partner amount
You can claim this credit if you supported your spouse or common-law partner during the tax
                            year. You must reduce the amount of the credit based on their net income.
If you are using certified tax software, it will ask you to confirm your marital status. The
                            software will prompt you to answer questions about your spouse or common-law partner. It
                            will then calculate (on a Schedule 5, Amounts for Spouse or Common-law Partner and
                            Dependants) the credit amount and enter it on the correct line.
Example: Claiming the spouse or common-law partner amount
Sean and Sam are in a common-law relationship. Sam had no income to report on his tax
                                return for last year. Sean can claim the full spouse or common-law partner amount since
                                Sam did not have any income.

#### Amount for an eligible dependant
You may, under certain conditions, be able to claim this credit if you supported a dependant
                            child or family member on your own. The amount of this credit is calculated using the
                            dependant’s net income.
A person who, at any time in the year, is dependent on you for support.
For this credit, the dependant must also have been either:
- your parent or grandparent by blood, marriage, common-law partnership, or adoption
- your child, grandchild, brother or sister by blood, marriage, common-law partnership, or adoption and was under 18 years of age or had an impairment in physical or mental functions
If you are using certified tax software, you will need to indicate if you have any
                            dependants. If you do, the software will then prompt you to answer questions to determine if
                            you are eligible. It will then calculate (on a Schedule 5, Amounts for Spouse or Common-law
                            Partner and Dependants) the credit amount and enter it on the correct line.
Example: Claiming the amount for an eligible dependant
Naya is single and lives alone with her one-year-old child. Naya can claim the full
                                amount for an eligible dependant because she is single, she supported her child in a
                                home that she maintained, and her child has no income. If the child has an income, Naya
                                would have to subtract that income from the amount for eligible dependant.

Form RC381, Inter-Provincial Calculation for CPP and QPP Contributions and Overpayments

You can claim this credit for eligible tuition fees paid to a recognized educational
                            institution. Generally, this includes post‑secondary institutions, such as universities or
                            colleges, or other educational institutions where you gain skills to develop your career,

You calculate the amount you can claim by using the information from the official tax receipt

                            Enrolment Certificate. The receipt will show the tuition paid, as well as how many months

You must report your tuition amounts in the year they were paid so that the Canada Revenue
                            Agency (CRA) can update your account. However, you may not need to use all your tuition
                            credits to reduce the taxes you owe that year. This could happen if you had a low income and
                            no taxes to pay.
If you don’t need to use all your tuition credits, you can choose to transfer unused credits
                            from the current tax year, up to the allowable maximum, to an eligible individual. This will
                            help reduce the taxes the other individual owes.
Whether or not you choose to transfer your unused tuition credits, you can carry forward any
                            unused credits until they are used up. You will need to claim those credits in the first
                            year that you owe taxes. This could be once you start working full-time after you have
                            graduated. Claiming these credits will reduce the taxes you owe on your income.
- your spouse or common-law partner

### How tax is calculated
Once you have established how much of your income is taxable, you will complete the federal
                            tax calculation in Step 5 of the income tax and benefit return.
This calculation uses your taxable income, the tax rates that apply, and the tax credits you
                            are entitled to claim. These amounts will determine how much federal taxes you might owe.
If you are using certified tax software, it will do the calculations for you automatically
                            and may not show the individual steps.

- − minus Federal

Note : Federal tax will show as "Net federal tax" on the paper return.
You will do a similar calculation to figure out the provincial or territorial taxes you
                                might owe.

- − minus Provincial or
                                    territorial non-refundable tax credits

### Determining the amount of federal tax on your taxable income
Canada has a progressive income tax system, which means you pay more tax as your
                                    income increases.
The taxes you pay are determined by which of the 5 tax brackets your income falls
                                    into. A different tax rate applies to each tax bracket but only to the income within
                                    that bracket. The higher tax rate does not apply to all your taxable income. The
                                    same tax brackets are used for all types of income, such as employment income or
                                    occasional earnings.

The next step is to calculate your non-refundable tax credits. You will then subtract your

                            personal amount is $16,129 for the 2025 tax year. In general, anyone with a taxable income

### Refund or balance owing
In Step 6 of your income tax and benefit return, you use two amounts to
                                determine the final result of your tax return.
If you are using certified tax software, it will do the calculations for you
                                automatically and may not show the separate steps.

#### Step 6: Refund or balance owing
- − minus Total credits
- = equals Refund or
                                    balance owing
The first amount is your total payable , which is the sum of all the
                                amounts you owe. This is usually made up of your federal and provincial or territorial
                                taxes.
Note : If you lived in Quebec on December 31 of the tax year, you will
                                calculate your provincial taxes by completing a separate provincial tax return for
                                Revenu Québec.
The second amount is your total credits , which includes your refundable
                                tax credits. Refundable tax credits reduce the amount you owe to zero first and, unlike
                                non-refundable tax credits, any remaining credit is refunded to you.
The difference between these amounts is known as your refund , where the
                                Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) owes you an amount, or your balance owing ,
                                where you must pay an amount to the CRA.
Some scammers may pretend to be the CRA and try to scare you into paying a debt that
                                    doesn’t really exist. The CRA will not use aggressive language or
                                    threaten you with arrest or sending the police.

### Common amounts included in your total credits

#### Total income tax deducted
This amount represents the total income tax deducted directly from any of your sources of
                                income. The most common example of this is the income tax your employer takes off of
                                your pay. It is shown on your information slips, such as in box 22 of your T4 slip.
If you are using certified tax software, you enter the amount shown on all of your
                                information slips, and the software will automatically enter the total on the correct
                                line.

#### Canada Pension Plan (CPP) overpayment
This is an amount you can claim if you contributed more to the CPP than you had to during
                                the year. This might happen if you worked for more than one employer during the year or
                                if your employer deducted CPP contributions when you were under 18 years of age, when
                                you were not required to contribute.
Certified tax software will automatically calculate how much you should have contributed
                                to the CPP based on your income and give you a credit for any overpayment.
If you were a resident of Quebec on December 31 of the tax year, and you contributed
                                    to the CPP or QPP, claim an overpayment on your provincial income tax return for
                                    Québec.

### More common amounts included in your total credits

#### Employment insurance (EI) overpayment
You can claim this amount if you contributed more to EI than you had to for the year.
                                This might happen if you worked for more than one employer during the year.
Certified tax software will automatically calculate how much you should have contributed
                                to EI based on your income and give you a credit for any overpayment.

#### Canada workers benefit (CWB)
This amount is intended to supplement the earnings of low‑income workers and to be a work
                                incentive for low-income Canadians. It includes a basic amount and a disability
                                supplement if you are eligible for the disability tax credit.
The amount you can expect to receive for CWB depends on your income
                                and where you live.
Each certified tax software is different. The software might automatically calculate this
                                credit using information you previously entered, like your working income or your
                                marital status, or you may need to indicate that you would like to claim it. The
                                software will then calculate (on a Schedule 6, Canada Workers Benefit) the amount you
                                are entitled to and enter it on the correct line.
